text: 0.2g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, nutrient payoff is the whole point.
3.9g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
0.9g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin c at 17% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.
minerals are basically not part of the case here.

pros first: good volume for the calories because that matters for satiety and general usefulness. easy to build meals around because a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. very low downside whole food because vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff.

the drawbacks next: energy density is very low because good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal because most vegetables are not complete by themselves. rarely carries a meal by itself because that is normal here, but still part of the tradeoff.
C tier. Best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
